item.title: The Supreme Court turns its back on 60 years of Black enfranchisement
item.description: Its decision in Louisiana vs Callais ignores the law and overturns precedent

item.title: Musk, SpaceX and Muscovite fever dreams
item.description: Tech titans are in many ways the intellectual heirs of the Soviet space programme

item.title: How to make sense of SpaceX’s nonsensical valuation
item.description: The market has never before had to price a stock so speculative yet so large

item.title: Burnham’s not the messiah. He’s old Labour’s last throw
item.description: Being a new face gets you a hearing but doesn’t change the economic picture

item.title: Generating tax revenues in an automated world
item.description: If AI destroys job markets, governments will need to make up the resulting shortfall in labour income tax receipts

item.title: How not to manage a global oil crisis
item.description: Despite urgent warnings, governments have failed to take the current energy shock seriously enough

item.title: Europe’s trade weaponry is useless without political will
item.description: To stand up to China, the EU needs courageous governments, not another Commission regulation

item.title: EMs don’t panic like they used to
item.description: There’s been convergence with DMs

item.title: Employers must listen to young people if they want to help
item.description: The vast majority of those not in education or employment are desperate to work but stuck in the ‘rejection economy’

item.title: Americans beware: markets can be out of sync with reality
item.description: We should avoid being lulled into complacency by economic conditions that are still reasonably solid

item.title: The new art of war is just as bloody as the old
item.description: Armed drones and AI have transformed the frontline into treacherous kill zones comparable to the first world war

item.title: Starmer’s crisis lands on UK housebuilders’ doorstep
item.description: The sector is not in crisis but does face a cyclical slowdown, with a lot of hot air already coming out of stock prices

item.title: The benefits of an investment bubble
item.description: UK telecoms sector shows how splurge of money can destroy shareholder value but deliver consumer gains

item.title: The crisis in Japan’s love affair with plastic
item.description: Knock-on effects of the Strait of Hormuz’s closure are becoming a source of economic consternation

item.title: Farage, crypto and the £5mn gift
item.description: His rightwing populist Reform UK is uniquely reliant on a crypto billionaire

item.title: M&S has won over fashionistas but not yet investors
item.description: Consumers rank it first among its peers in the UK on value and style, but its shares have been falling

item.title: Blackstone’s $5bn data centre plan melds creativity and necessity
item.description: The idea makes sense, and the opportunity is large

item.title: The Arctic’s vanishing caribou
item.description: While Canadians argue about who is to blame, herd numbers continue to fall

item.title: Labour has all too clear a vision
item.description: The accusation of vagueness does not fit this straightforwardly leftwing government

item.title: The oil shock is coming for America
item.description: Tools that worked to lower prices in the last crisis have already been spent

item.title: How can we ensure our children inherit equal amounts?
item.description: My wife and I have children from previous marriages and worry about what will happen once one of us dies

item.title: Why the BoE will not ride to the rescue in gilts
item.description: Plus, Asian chipmakers

item.title: Stock investors are high on hope and low on cash
item.description: There is plenty of opportunity for minor and major snafus given the S&P 500’s reliance on AI-driven companies

item.title: Merger spirits could lift the UK’s languishing mid-caps
item.description: FTSE 250 index makes it well suited to active investing — and opportunistic dealmaking

item.title: The energy crisis may just be starting
item.description: Oil futures markets are sanguine, but history shows expectations have often been disappointed
